enrichment.short_description: Udio is an AI music generation platform that turns short text prompts into full, radio-quality songs complete with vocals, lyrics, and instrumentation in under a minute.
enrichment.seo_title: Udio Review 2026: AI Music Generator Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Udio is an AI text-to-music generator that creates full songs from a prompt. See how it works, who built it, and its 2026 pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Udio is an AI-powered music generation platform that converts text prompts into complete, produced songs, including lyrics, vocals, and instrumentation, in roughly 30 to 40 seconds per generation. It was founded in 2023 by Yaroslav Ganin and David Ding, along with a team of former Google DeepMind researchers, and came out of stealth in April 2024 with a 10 million dollar seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz, backed by investors including musicians will.i.am and Common, Instagram co-founder Mike Krieger, and the distribution platform UnitedMasters. Udio is headquartered in New York City. The product lets users generate songs across genres by describing a style, mood, or lyrical theme, then extend, remix, or edit sections, add vocal styles, use audio-to-song conversion from a hummed or recorded reference, and generate custom cover art. Udio operates on a freemium credit-based model: a free tier offers 10 credits per day (up to 100 per month) capped at three full 2 minute 10 second songs daily for non-commercial use, while the Standard plan (10 dollars per month, or 8 dollars per month billed annually) unlocks 2,400 monthly credits, commercial usage rights, voice control, and style references, and the Pro plan (30 dollars per month, or 24 dollars per month annually) provides 6,000 monthly credits and support for up to 10 simultaneous song generations. Extra pay-as-you-go credits can be purchased separately and do not expire. Udio competes directly with Suno, another leading AI music generator, and has drawn significant industry attention, including a 2024 copyright infringement lawsuit filed by major record labels (Universal Music Group, Sony Music, and Warner Music) alleging its training data used copyrighted recordings without authorization. Udio has since reached a licensing settlement framework discussion with UMG, distinguishing it from Suno on the litigation timeline.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Udio
paragraphs: Udio is a generative AI platform that creates full-length, studio-quality songs from a simple text description. Users type a prompt describing a genre, mood, instrumentation, or lyrical theme, and Udio's models generate a complete track with sung vocals, melody, and arrangement, typically in well under a minute.

The company was founded in 2023 by Yaroslav Ganin and David Ding, both former machine learning researchers at Google DeepMind, and launched publicly in April 2024 backed by a 10 million dollar seed round led by Andreessen Horowitz with participation from prominent musicians and music-industry figures.

Udio positions itself as a creative songwriting and production tool for musicians, content creators, and hobbyists rather than a passive listening service, emphasizing editing and remixing controls on top of raw generation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core generation lets users produce two-minute-plus songs from text prompts, choosing genre, tempo, and vocal style, with the option to extend a track section by section.
Audio-to-song functionality allows uploading a hummed melody, recorded instrument, or existing audio clip as a reference that Udio turns into a fully arranged song.

Editing tools support inpainting specific sections, remixing existing generations, adjusting vocal characteristics, and applying style references drawn from other tracks, alongside automatic custom cover art generation for finished songs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Udio uses a credit-based freemium model. The Free plan gives 10 credits per day (100 per month, no rollover) capped at three full songs daily. The Standard plan is 10 dollars per month (8 dollars per month billed annually) for 2,400 monthly credits and commercial rights, and the Pro plan is 30 dollars per month (24 dollars per month billed annually) for 6,000 monthly credits and the highest concurrency.
Additional pay-as-you-go credit packs (for example 100 credits for 3 dollars) can be purchased on top of any plan and, unlike the monthly allotment, do not expire.

features.description: Generate a complete song with lyrics, vocals, and instrumentation from a short text prompt.

features.description: Extend generated tracks section by section to build out longer compositions.

features.description: Edit specific sections of a generated song or remix existing tracks.

features.title: Voice control
features.description: Adjust and steer vocal style and characteristics on paid plans.

features.title: Style references
features.description: Use another track as a style reference to guide genre and production choices.

features.title: Custom cover art
features.description: Automatically generate matching cover artwork for finished songs.

features.description: Standard and Pro subscribers can use generated songs commercially, unlike the free tier.

pros: Produces full, radio-quality songs with vocals in under a minute
Strong editing and remixing controls beyond one-shot generation
Backed by well-known investors and music-industry figures
Affordable entry-level paid tier compared to studio production costs
Audio-to-song feature is useful for turning rough musical ideas into finished tracks
cons: Facing music-industry copyright litigation over training data
Monthly credits do not roll over on paid plans
Free tier is capped at three songs per day and restricted to non-commercial use
Output quality and originality can be inconsistent across genres
No native mobile app; browser-based only

faqs.question: What is Udio used for?
faqs.answer: Udio generates complete songs, including vocals and instrumentation, from text prompts, letting musicians and creators produce demos, backing tracks, or finished songs quickly.

faqs.question: Who founded Udio?
faqs.answer: Udio was founded in 2023 by Yaroslav Ganin and David Ding, former Google DeepMind researchers, and launched publicly in April 2024.

faqs.question: Is Udio free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Udio has a free tier with 10 credits per day and up to three full songs daily, though usage is limited to non-commercial purposes.

faqs.question: Can I use Udio songs commercially?
faqs.answer: Commercial usage rights are included on the paid Standard and Pro plans, not on the free tier.

faqs.question: How much does Udio cost?
faqs.answer: Standard is 10 dollars per month (8 dollars per month billed annually) and Pro is 30 dollars per month (24 dollars per month billed annually).

faqs.question: Is Udio involved in any lawsuits?
faqs.answer: Yes, major record labels including Universal Music Group, Sony Music, and Warner Music filed a copyright infringement lawsuit against Udio in 2024 over training data.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Udio and Suno?
faqs.answer: Both are AI music generators; Udio is generally noted for stronger editing and remix controls, while Suno is a longer-established competitor with a larger user base.

faqs.question: Does Udio offer an API?
faqs.answer: Udio has not launched a broadly available public API; access is primarily through its web application.

enrichment.short_description: UKG Pro is an enterprise human capital management suite covering payroll, benefits, talent management, time and attendance, and workforce analytics for mid-size to large organizations.
enrichment.headquarters: Weston, Florida and Lowell, Massachusetts, United States
enrichment.seo_title: UKG Pro Review 2026: HCM Features, Pricing and Modules
enrichment.seo_meta_description: UKG Pro is an enterprise HCM suite for payroll, HR, and talent management. See its features, pricing model, and how it fits mid-to-large companies.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: UKG Pro is the flagship human capital management (HCM) product of UKG (Ultimate Kronos Group), a company formed in 2020 through the merger of Ultimate Software, founded in Florida in 1990, and Kronos Incorporated, founded in Massachusetts in 1977. UKG maintains dual headquarters in Weston, Florida and Lowell, Massachusetts, and reports roughly 15,000 to 17,000 employees company-wide as of 2025-2026. UKG Pro is a cloud-based, modular suite aimed at mid-size to large enterprises, generally organizations with 350 to 10,000 employees, and includes core HR and employee records, payroll and tax administration, benefits administration, time and attendance, workforce scheduling, recruiting and applicant tracking, onboarding, performance and compensation management, succession planning, employee engagement surveys, and reporting and analytics with workforce-planning forecasting tools. It is distinct from UKG Ready, UKG's separate product line built for small and mid-size businesses on a different platform architecture. UKG Pro is sold on a subscription basis, typically billed quarterly or annually, with published estimates placing per-employee-per-month (PEPM) costs around 27 to 37 dollars depending on the modules selected, plus a one-time implementation fee that industry sources estimate at 40 to 70 percent of the first year's software fees; UKG does not publish list pricing and requires a custom quote. UKG Pro competes with Workday, ADP Workforce Now, Oracle HCM Cloud, Ceridian Dayforce, and BambooHR (for smaller companies), and is generally positioned as a comprehensive, configurable, enterprise-grade alternative for companies that need deep payroll compliance and talent management in a single system rather than a lighter point solution.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is UKG Pro
paragraphs: UKG Pro is a cloud-based human capital management (HCM) suite built for mid-size to large enterprises, combining payroll, HR, talent management, time and attendance, and workforce analytics in one platform.

It is a product of UKG (Ultimate Kronos Group), formed in 2020 when Ultimate Software (founded 1990) merged with Kronos Incorporated (founded 1977). UKG Pro descends from Ultimate Software's UltiPro platform and is UKG's higher-end, enterprise-focused offering, distinct from UKG Ready, which targets smaller organizations.

The suite is designed around a single employee record shared across payroll, benefits, talent, and time modules, aiming to reduce duplicate data entry and give HR teams a unified view of the workforce.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: UKG Pro covers the full hire-to-retire lifecycle: recruiting and applicant tracking, onboarding, core HR and employee self-service, benefits administration, payroll and tax compliance, time and attendance, scheduling, performance and compensation management, succession planning, and employee engagement surveys.

Workforce planning tools help HR and finance teams forecast labor demand, model headcount scenarios, and analyze labor cost trends, while built-in compliance automation helps track changing HR, payroll, and employment regulations across jurisdictions.

Reporting and analytics dashboards give role-based access to payroll, turnover, and engagement data, and the platform supports integrations with third-party benefits carriers, background-check vendors, and other HR point solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: UKG Pro is sold through custom, quote-based enterprise contracts rather than published list pricing, typically billed quarterly or annually on a per-employee-per-month (PEPM) basis.

Industry estimates place UKG Pro's PEPM cost around 27 to 37 dollars depending on which modules (payroll, talent, benefits, time) are bundled, with an additional one-time implementation fee that can run 40 to 70 percent of the first year's subscription cost.

Strong payroll compliance capabilities for complex, multi-jurisdiction organizations
Deep talent management modules including succession and compensation planning

Long operating history through Ultimate Software and Kronos heritage
cons: Pricing is opaque and requires a custom sales quote
Implementation fees can be substantial relative to annual subscription cost
Complexity and configuration depth can mean a longer, more resource-intensive rollout
Best suited to mid-to-large enterprises; overkill for very small businesses (UKG Ready targets that segment instead)

faqs.question: What is UKG Pro?
faqs.answer: UKG Pro is UKG's enterprise human capital management suite covering payroll, HR, benefits, time and attendance, and talent management for mid-size to large organizations.

faqs.question: What company makes UKG Pro?
faqs.answer: UKG Pro is made by UKG (Ultimate Kronos Group), formed in 2020 from the merger of Ultimate Software and Kronos Incorporated.

faqs.question: How is UKG Pro different from UKG Ready?
faqs.answer: UKG Pro is built for mid-size to large enterprises, generally 350 to 10,000 employees, while UKG Ready is a separate product on a different platform aimed at small and mid-size businesses.

faqs.question: How much does UKG Pro cost?
faqs.answer: UKG does not publish list pricing; industry estimates put UKG Pro around 27 to 37 dollars per employee per month plus an implementation fee, based on a custom quote.

faqs.question: What company size is UKG Pro built for?
faqs.answer: UKG Pro is generally positioned for organizations with roughly 350 to 10,000 employees.

faqs.question: Does UKG Pro handle payroll?
faqs.answer: Yes, payroll processing and multi-jurisdiction tax compliance are core UKG Pro modules.

faqs.question: What are UKG Pro's main competitors?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Workday, ADP Workforce Now, Oracle HCM Cloud, and Ceridian Dayforce.

faqs.question: Where is UKG headquartered?
faqs.answer: UKG maintains dual headquarters in Weston, Florida and Lowell, Massachusetts.

enrichment.short_description: Ultralytics is the company behind the YOLO family of open-source computer vision models, offering object detection, segmentation, and pose estimation plus a hosted platform for training and deploying them.
enrichment.seo_title: Ultralytics Review 2026: YOLO Computer Vision Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ultralytics builds the open-source YOLO computer vision models. See YOLO11/YOLO26 features, AGPL licensing, and Ultralytics Hub pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ultralytics is a computer vision company founded in 2014 by Glenn Jocher, who remains its CEO, headquartered in Frederick, Maryland, with a distributed team reported at roughly 50 to 60 employees across multiple continents as of 2026. The company is best known as the maintainer of the YOLO (You Only Look Once) family of real-time object detection models, including YOLOv5, YOLOv8, YOLO11, and the newer YOLO26, which are widely used in academic research, industrial inspection, robotics, autonomous vehicles, and video analytics for tasks such as object detection, instance segmentation, pose estimation, image classification, and object tracking. The core Ultralytics YOLO codebase is open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, meaning any product built on it and distributed to others must also release its full source code, unless the user purchases an Ultralytics Enterprise License, which allows embedding YOLO models in proprietary, closed-source commercial products without open-sourcing the larger application. Beyond the open-source library, Ultralytics operates Ultralytics Hub (referred to as the Ultralytics Platform), a cloud service for dataset management, model training, and one-click deployment across 17-plus export formats. Hub pricing includes a free tier with unlimited public and private projects, 100 models, three concurrent cloud trainings, and 100 GB of storage; a Pro tier at 29 dollars per seat per month (290 dollars per year) with 500 models, 10 concurrent trainings, and 500 GB storage; and a custom-priced Enterprise tier bundling the commercial Enterprise License with dedicated support. Ultralytics competes with Roboflow, Google's MediaPipe, and other open computer-vision toolkits, and is distinguished by YOLO's status as one of the most widely cited and deployed real-time object-detection architectures in the field.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Ultralytics
paragraphs: Ultralytics is a computer vision company founded in 2014 by Glenn Jocher, best known for developing and maintaining the YOLO (You Only Look Once) family of real-time object detection models, one of the most widely used architectures in applied computer vision.
The company is headquartered in Frederick, Maryland, and operates as a distributed team across North America, Europe, and Asia, with roughly 50 to 60 employees as of 2026.

Ultralytics provides both an open-source Python library for training and running YOLO models locally, and Ultralytics Hub, a hosted platform for dataset management, cloud training, and model deployment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The YOLO model family supports object detection, instance segmentation, pose estimation, oriented bounding box detection, image classification, and multi-object tracking, with successive generations (YOLOv5, YOLOv8, YOLO11, YOLO26) improving speed and accuracy.

Ultralytics Hub adds dataset annotation tools including automated SAM (Segment Anything Model) assistance, cloud-based model training with configurable compute, and one-click export to 17-plus deployment formats such as ONNX, TensorRT, and CoreML.
Models can be deployed to edge devices, mobile, cloud endpoints, or embedded systems, and the library integrates with common MLOps tools for experiment tracking and versioning.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Ultralytics YOLO codebase is free and open source under AGPL-3.0, which requires derivative works distributed to others to also be open-sourced. Organizations that want to embed YOLO in closed-source commercial products need an Enterprise License, priced individually based on usage scope.

Ultralytics Hub adds a separate SaaS pricing tier on top of the open-source library: a free plan with core training and storage limits, a Pro plan at 29 dollars per seat per month (290 dollars per year with annual billing), and a custom Enterprise plan bundling the commercial license with dedicated support.

pros: Widely adopted, well-documented open-source object detection framework
Fast, actively maintained model releases with strong community support
Flexible deployment across edge, mobile, and cloud environments
Free tier of Ultralytics Hub is usable for real projects, not just a trial
Broad export format support simplifies production deployment
cons: AGPL-3.0 licensing requires open-sourcing derivative commercial products unless an Enterprise License is purchased
Enterprise License pricing is not published and requires direct negotiation
Training large models still requires meaningful GPU compute, which adds cost on Hub's paid tiers
Smaller company size relative to some competitors may mean slower enterprise support response

faqs.question: What is Ultralytics known for?
faqs.answer: Ultralytics is best known for developing and maintaining the YOLO family of real-time object detection and computer vision models.

faqs.question: Is Ultralytics YOLO free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core YOLO codebase is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, though commercial closed-source use requires an Enterprise License.

faqs.question: Who founded Ultralytics?
faqs.answer: Ultralytics was founded in 2014 by Glenn Jocher, who serves as its CEO.

faqs.question: What is the difference between YOLOv8 and YOLO11?
faqs.answer: YOLO11 is a newer generation of the YOLO architecture released after YOLOv8, offering improved accuracy and efficiency; both are maintained and distributed by Ultralytics.

faqs.question: What is Ultralytics Hub?
faqs.answer: Ultralytics Hub (the Ultralytics Platform) is a cloud service for dataset management, model training, and deployment built on top of the open-source YOLO library.

faqs.question: How much does Ultralytics Hub Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Ultralytics Hub Pro costs 29 dollars per seat per month, or 290 dollars per year with annual billing.

faqs.question: Do I need an Enterprise License to use YOLO commercially?
faqs.answer: You need an Enterprise License if you want to embed YOLO in a proprietary, closed-source product without complying with AGPL-3.0's open-source requirements.

faqs.question: Where is Ultralytics headquartered?
faqs.answer: Ultralytics is headquartered in Frederick, Maryland, United States.

enrichment.short_description: Ulysses is a Mac, iPad, and iPhone writing app built for long-form authors, offering a distraction-free Markdown editor with library organization and export to multiple formats.
enrichment.seo_title: Ulysses Review 2026: Mac and iOS Writing App Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Ulysses is a distraction-free Markdown writing app for Mac, iPad, and iPhone. See its features, subscription pricing, and who it is built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Ulysses is a long-form writing application built exclusively for Apple platforms, macOS, iPadOS, and iOS, developed by The Soulmen, a small German independent software studio. The app has been in development since 2003 and is aimed at authors, journalists, bloggers, and academics who write long documents such as novels, articles, and theses. Ulysses organizes writing into a searchable library of sheets and groups rather than loose files, uses a lightweight Markup syntax (a Markdown variant) for formatting so writers can stay in a distraction-free plain-text environment, and exports finished work to formats including PDF, ePub, Word (.docx), HTML, and direct publishing to WordPress and Medium. In August 2017, Ulysses moved from a one-time purchase model to a subscription, a widely discussed shift covered by outlets like 9to5Mac, TidBITS, and Cult of Mac at the time. As of 2026, pricing is 5.99 dollars per month or 49.99 dollars per year, with a discounted 34.99 dollar annual education rate for verified students and educators, and legacy customers who purchased before the subscription switch retain a grandfathered discounted renewal rate around 30 dollars per year. A single subscription covers Mac, iPad, and iPhone, and Apple Family Sharing extends access to up to five family members. Ulysses has no Windows, Android, or web version, making it a Mac-ecosystem-only tool, which distinguishes it from cross-platform competitors like Scrivener, iA Writer, and Notion. Its appeal centers on a polished writing-focused interface, reliable iCloud/Mac sync, and a workflow purpose-built for drafting and exporting long manuscripts rather than general note-taking or collaboration.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Ulysses
paragraphs: Ulysses is a writing application built exclusively for Mac, iPad, and iPhone, designed for authors working on long-form content such as novels, articles, blog posts, and academic papers.
It is developed by The Soulmen, a small independent software studio based in Germany, with the app's development history dating back to 2003.

Rather than organizing work as loose files, Ulysses stores writing in a searchable library of sheets and groups, and uses a Markdown-based syntax so writers can format text without leaving the keyboard or seeing visual clutter.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The library system organizes drafts into groups and sheets with tags, filters, and full-text search, making it easier to manage book-length projects with many chapters or a large archive of articles.

Ulysses supports a lightweight Markup syntax for headings, emphasis, links, and footnotes, along with goal-setting for word or character counts, a typewriter mode, and customizable themes for distraction-free writing.
Export and publishing options include PDF, ePub, Word (.docx), HTML, and direct publishing integrations with WordPress and Medium, alongside seamless iCloud sync across Mac, iPad, and iPhone.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Ulysses is subscription-based at 5.99 dollars per month or 49.99 dollars per year, covering use across all supported Apple devices with a single account, and includes a fully functional free trial.

Verified students and educators receive a discounted 34.99 dollar annual rate, and users who purchased Ulysses before its 2017 switch from one-time pricing to subscriptions retain a grandfathered renewal rate around 30 dollars per year.

Discounted education pricing and Family Sharing support

faqs.question: What is Ulysses used for?
faqs.answer: Ulysses is a distraction-free writing app for drafting long-form content like novels, articles, and academic papers on Mac, iPad, and iPhone.

faqs.question: Is Ulysses available on Windows?
faqs.answer: No, Ulysses is exclusive to Apple platforms and has no Windows, Android, or web version.

faqs.question: How much does Ulysses cost?
faqs.answer: Ulysses costs 5.99 dollars per month or 49.99 dollars per year, with a discounted 34.99 dollar annual rate for students and educators.

faqs.question: Who makes Ulysses?
faqs.answer: Ulysses is developed by The Soulmen, a small independent software studio based in Germany.

faqs.question: Did Ulysses used to be a one-time purchase?

faqs.answer: Yes, a single Ulysses subscription covers Mac, iPad, and iPhone with iCloud sync between devices.

enrichment.short_description: umami is an open-source, privacy-focused web analytics platform that can be self-hosted for free or used as a hosted cloud service, positioned as a lightweight Google Analytics alternative.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted); Cloud from free, Pro plans available
enrichment.seo_title: umami Review 2026: Open-Source Privacy Analytics Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: umami is an open-source, privacy-first web analytics tool. Compare self-hosted vs Umami Cloud pricing, features, and how it works as a GA alternative.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: umami is an open-source, privacy-focused website analytics platform created by Mike Cao, who built the first version in late 2020 after wanting a faster, simpler, cookie-free alternative to Google Analytics for his own personal websites and open-sourced it immediately. Umami Software, Inc., co-founded with Brian Cao and Francis Cao, was later established around the project and raised 1.5 million dollars in a pre-seed funding round led by Race Capital in July 2022 to grow the open-source platform and its hosted cloud offering. The codebase is MIT-licensed and free to self-host indefinitely, tracking pageviews, visitors, referrers, UTM campaigns, custom events, and goals without using cookies, which allows it to operate in many jurisdictions without a cookie consent banner while remaining GDPR- and CCPA-friendly. For teams that do not want to manage their own server and database, Umami Cloud offers a hosted version: a free Hobby tier supporting roughly 100,000 events per month across a limited number of sites, and a paid Pro tier priced around 20 dollars per month for higher event volumes (roughly 1 million events per month), more sites, longer data retention, and email reports, with custom Enterprise pricing available on request. umami's dashboard emphasizes simplicity: a single-page overview of traffic, top pages, referrers, devices, and countries, an approach that contrasts with the deeper but more complex reporting in Google Analytics 4 or paid competitors like Plausible, Fathom, and Simple Analytics. Because it is open source, umami can also be self-hosted on infrastructure like Vercel, Railway, Docker, or a VPS, giving technical teams full data ownership at no licensing cost, which is its core differentiator from closed-source privacy analytics competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is umami
paragraphs: umami is an open-source web analytics tool that tracks website traffic without cookies or personal data collection, built as a simpler, privacy-respecting alternative to Google Analytics.

It was created by Mike Cao in late 2020, initially to replace his own use of Google Analytics on personal projects, and was open-sourced from the start. Umami Software, Inc. later formalized the project as a company and raised 1.5 million dollars in pre-seed funding from Race Capital in 2022.
The project is MIT-licensed, meaning anyone can self-host it for free indefinitely, while Umami Cloud offers a managed hosted version for teams that prefer not to run their own server.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: umami tracks pageviews, unique visitors, sessions, referrers, UTM campaign parameters, browser, device, and country-level data, along with custom events and conversion goal tracking, all without setting cookies.
The dashboard is intentionally minimal, presenting key metrics on a single screen instead of the deep, multi-report structure found in tools like Google Analytics 4.

Because it does not rely on cookies, umami can generally operate without triggering cookie consent banner requirements in many regions, and self-hosted deployments keep all visitor data under the site owner's control.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Self-hosting umami is completely free under its open-source MIT license; users only pay for their own hosting infrastructure.

Umami Cloud offers a free Hobby tier (around 100,000 events per month, limited sites) and a paid Pro tier priced around 20 dollars per month for roughly 1 million events per month, more sites, and longer data retention, with custom Enterprise pricing available for larger needs.

cons: Self-hosting requires managing your own server, database, and updates
Reporting depth is more limited than Google Analytics 4 or enterprise analytics tools
Umami Cloud paid tiers add cost as event volume grows

faqs.question: Is umami really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, umami is free and open source under the MIT license when self-hosted; Umami Cloud offers a free tier plus paid tiers for higher usage.

faqs.question: Who created umami?
faqs.answer: umami was created by Mike Cao in late 2020, later formalized as Umami Software, Inc. with co-founders Brian Cao and Francis Cao.

faqs.question: Is umami a Google Analytics alternative?
faqs.answer: Yes, umami is commonly used as a lightweight, privacy-focused alternative to Google Analytics.

faqs.question: Does umami use cookies?
faqs.answer: No, umami tracks visitors without cookies, which helps many sites avoid cookie consent banner requirements.

faqs.question: Can I self-host umami?
faqs.answer: Yes, umami is open source and can be self-hosted on your own server, VPS, or platforms like Docker, Vercel, or Railway.

faqs.question: How much does Umami Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Umami Cloud has a free Hobby tier for lower traffic sites and a paid Pro tier priced around 20 dollars per month for higher event volumes.

faqs.question: Is umami GDPR compliant?
faqs.answer: umami's cookie-free, privacy-focused design is built to align with GDPR and CCPA principles, though compliance ultimately depends on overall site configuration.

faqs.question: What license is umami released under?
faqs.answer: umami's source code is released under the MIT open-source license.

enrichment.short_description: Umbraco is a free, open-source .NET content management system used to build and manage websites, with an optional managed Umbraco Cloud hosting service for teams.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Umbraco is a free, open-source .NET CMS. See its history, features, MIT license, and Umbraco Cloud pricing plans for 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Umbraco is an open-source content management system built on the Microsoft .NET (ASP.NET) platform, originally developed by Niels Hartvig starting around 2000 and released publicly as open-source software in 2004, becoming fully open under Umbraco 2.0 in February 2005; the codebase has been available under an MIT license since version 4.5. The company behind it, Umbraco A/S, is headquartered in Odense, Denmark, and opened a United States headquarters in Charlotte, North Carolina in 2020 to support its growing American customer base; the company reports several hundred employees globally. Umbraco is used to power more than 500,000 live websites and has a community of over 220,000 active members, making it one of the most widely deployed .NET-based open-source CMS platforms. The core Umbraco CMS is free to download, self-host, and modify under its MIT license, while the company generates revenue primarily through Umbraco Cloud, a managed hosting and DevOps service that adds automated deployments, backups, and scaling on top of the open-source CMS. Umbraco Cloud is tiered into a Starter plan (around 55 dollars per month, aimed at low-traffic, low-complexity sites on shared infrastructure), a Standard plan (around 330 dollars per month), a Professional plan (around 900 dollars per month), and a custom-quoted Enterprise plan with dedicated resources and SLAs. Umbraco competes with other .NET and open-source CMS platforms such as Sitecore, Kentico, and Optimizely, as well as broader CMS alternatives like WordPress, and is generally chosen by organizations already invested in the Microsoft development stack that want an open-source, developer-friendly content platform rather than a proprietary enterprise CMS license.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Umbraco
paragraphs: Umbraco is a free, open-source content management system built on Microsoft's .NET (ASP.NET) framework, used by developers to build and manage websites and intranets.

It was created by Niels Hartvig, who began development around 2000 and released Umbraco as open source in 2004, with the CMS becoming fully open source at version 2.0 in 2005. Since version 4.5 the codebase has been licensed under MIT.

The company behind Umbraco, Umbraco A/S, is headquartered in Odense, Denmark, with a U.S. headquarters opened in Charlotte, North Carolina in 2020, and more than 500,000 live websites currently run on Umbraco.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Umbraco provides a flexible content editing interface built around customizable document types, allowing developers to define exactly what content editors can create and edit.

It supports a headless/hybrid delivery model through its Content Delivery API, multi-site and multi-language content management, member and role-based access for building portals or gated content, and a large marketplace of community and commercial packages that extend functionality.

As a .NET application, Umbraco integrates naturally with Microsoft Azure hosting, Visual Studio tooling, and existing enterprise .NET codebases, which is a key reason it is favored by organizations already using the Microsoft stack.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core Umbraco CMS is free and open source under the MIT license, with no cost to download, self-host, or modify the code.

Umbraco Cloud, the company's managed hosting service, is tiered at roughly 55 dollars per month for Starter, 330 dollars per month for Standard, 900 dollars per month for Professional, and custom Enterprise pricing for dedicated infrastructure and SLAs.

Strong fit for organizations already using the .NET/Microsoft stack
Large, active community and package ecosystem after two decades of development
Flexible content modeling suited to complex, custom site architectures
Optional managed Umbraco Cloud simplifies hosting and deployment
cons: Requires .NET development expertise, less accessible to non-developers than page-builder CMSs
Umbraco Cloud pricing rises quickly at higher tiers (Professional around 900 dollars/month)
Smaller plugin ecosystem than WordPress
Self-hosting still requires managing .NET application hosting infrastructure

faqs.question: Is Umbraco free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core Umbraco CMS is free and open source under the MIT license; Umbraco Cloud managed hosting is a separate paid service.

faqs.question: Who created Umbraco?
faqs.answer: Umbraco was created by Niels Hartvig, who began development around 2000 and open-sourced it in 2004.

faqs.question: What programming language is Umbraco built in?
faqs.answer: Umbraco is built in C# on Microsoft's .NET (ASP.NET) framework.

faqs.question: Where is Umbraco headquartered?
faqs.answer: Umbraco A/S is headquartered in Odense, Denmark, with a U.S. headquarters in Charlotte, North Carolina.

faqs.question: How much does Umbraco Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Umbraco Cloud starts around 55 dollars per month for the Starter plan, rising to around 330 for Standard, 900 for Professional, and custom pricing for Enterprise.

faqs.question: How many websites run on Umbraco?
faqs.answer: More than 500,000 live websites run on Umbraco CMS and Umbraco Cloud.

faqs.question: Is Umbraco headless?
faqs.answer: Umbraco supports headless and hybrid delivery through its Content Delivery API, in addition to traditional server-rendered pages.

faqs.question: What license does Umbraco use?
faqs.answer: Umbraco has been available under the MIT open-source license since version 4.5.

enrichment.short_description: Umbrel is a free, open-source personal server operating system (umbrelOS) with a one-click app store, letting anyone self-host apps like Nextcloud, Plex, and a Bitcoin node on a home server.
enrichment.seo_title: Umbrel Review 2026: Self-Hosted Home Server OS Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Umbrel is a free, open-source home server OS with a one-click app store. See umbrelOS features, self-hosted apps, and Umbrel Home hardware pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Umbrel is an open-source personal cloud operating system, umbrelOS, that turns a Raspberry Pi, old PC, or dedicated device into a self-hosted home server with a one-click app store. It was founded in 2020 by Mayank Chhabra and Luke Childs, who set out to make self-hosting accessible to non-technical users by wrapping Docker-based apps in a simple, mobile-friendly interface. The umbrelOS software and its underlying app store infrastructure are free and open source, with no subscription fees; users install it on their own hardware (commonly a Raspberry Pi 4/5) at no software cost. The Umbrel App Store offers one-click installation for more than 300 self-hosted applications, including Nextcloud for file storage, Plex and Jellyfin for media streaming, Home Assistant for smart home control, a Bitcoin full node and Lightning node, password managers, and various developer tools, positioning Umbrel as an alternative to relying on third-party cloud services like Google Drive, Dropbox, or Spotify for people who want to own and control their own data. In 2023, the company began selling Umbrel Home, a plug-and-play pre-built server appliance priced at 699 dollars, featuring a quad-core Intel CPU, 16GB of RAM, a 2TB NVMe SSD, and low power draw (around 10 watts), aimed at users who want turnkey hardware without assembling their own Raspberry Pi setup. Umbrel has raised early-stage venture funding and reports modest annual recurring revenue in the low millions as of 2024-2025 estimates from third-party data providers, reflecting its niche but growing presence in the self-hosting and home server community alongside comparable projects like CasaOS, TrueNAS, and Unraid.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Umbrel
paragraphs: Umbrel is a free, open-source operating system called umbrelOS that transforms a Raspberry Pi, spare PC, or dedicated device into a personal home server, giving users a simple app store to self-host their own cloud services.

It was founded in 2020 by Mayank Chhabra and Luke Childs, aiming to make self-hosting accessible to people without deep technical expertise by wrapping Docker containers in a friendly, one-click interface.
In 2023, Umbrel began selling Umbrel Home, a plug-and-play hardware appliance for people who want a ready-made server instead of assembling their own.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Umbrel App Store offers one-click installation of more than 300 self-hosted applications, including Nextcloud for file sync and storage, Plex and Jellyfin for media streaming, Home Assistant for smart home automation, and a Bitcoin and Lightning node.
umbrelOS provides a clean, mobile-responsive dashboard for managing installed apps, storage, backups, and remote access without needing command-line or Docker expertise.
Because it runs on the user's own hardware, all data stays local, giving people an alternative to depending on third-party cloud services like Google Drive, Dropbox, or streaming subscriptions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: umbrelOS itself is completely free and open source, with no subscription fee; users only need compatible hardware, most commonly a Raspberry Pi 4 or 5, an old PC, or a home server.
For users who want ready-made hardware, Umbrel sells Umbrel Home, a pre-built plug-and-play server appliance priced at 699 dollars, with financing options available through installment plans.

cons: Requires owning and maintaining physical hardware

Reliability and uptime depend on the user's home network and power stability

Some advanced apps still require basic technical troubleshooting

faqs.question: Is Umbrel free?
faqs.answer: Yes, umbrelOS and its app store are free and open source; users only pay for their own hardware or the optional Umbrel Home appliance.

faqs.question: What is Umbrel used for?
faqs.answer: Umbrel is used to self-host apps like file storage, media servers, smart home automation, and a Bitcoin node on a personal home server.

faqs.question: Who founded Umbrel?
faqs.answer: Umbrel was founded in 2020 by Mayank Chhabra and Luke Childs.

faqs.question: What hardware does Umbrel run on?
faqs.answer: umbrelOS commonly runs on a Raspberry Pi 4 or 5, an old PC repurposed as a server, or the company's own Umbrel Home appliance.

faqs.question: What is Umbrel Home?
faqs.answer: Umbrel Home is a pre-built, plug-and-play server appliance sold by Umbrel for 699 dollars, designed for users who want ready-made hardware.

faqs.answer: The Umbrel App Store offers one-click installation for more than 300 self-hosted applications.

faqs.question: Does Umbrel require technical skills to set up?
faqs.answer: Umbrel is designed to be approachable for non-technical users, though some familiarity with home networking is helpful.

faqs.question: Is Umbrel the same as Umbraco?
faqs.answer: No, Umbrel is a self-hosted personal server operating system, while Umbraco is an unrelated open-source .NET content management system.

enrichment.short_description: Unbounce is a landing page builder and conversion optimization platform that lets marketers create, test, and publish landing pages, popups, and sticky bars without coding.
enrichment.seo_title: Unbounce Review 2026: Landing Page Builder Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Unbounce is a no-code landing page builder with A/B testing and AI optimization. See its 2026 pricing plans, features, and who it is built for.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Unbounce is a landing page building and conversion optimization platform founded in 2009 in Vancouver, Canada, by Rick Perreault, Carl Schmidt, Oli Gardner, and Jason Murphy, created to let marketers build and test landing pages without needing developers. The company is headquartered in Vancouver and grew into one of the earliest dedicated landing page tools in the martech category; in 2021, private equity firm Thoma Bravo acquired a majority stake in Unbounce, valuing the company at an estimated enterprise value exceeding 800 million dollars, and the company has continued operating under that ownership with leadership changes including CEO Felicia Bochicchio taking over in 2023. Unbounce's core product is a drag-and-drop, no-code builder for landing pages, popups, and sticky bars, paired with built-in A/B testing and its Smart Traffic feature, an AI-driven tool that automatically routes visitors to the page variant statistically most likely to convert them, learning from real-time visitor data rather than waiting for a test to reach full statistical significance. As of 2026, Unbounce is priced across three core tiers: Build at 99 dollars per month (around 64 dollars per month billed annually) for unlimited landing pages and popups up to 20,000 monthly visitors; Experiment at 149 dollars per month for unlimited A/B testing and variants; and Optimize at 249 dollars per month, which adds Smart Traffic and advanced targeting like location and cookie-based targeting, supporting up to 50,000 monthly unique visitors. All plans include a 14-day free trial. Unbounce competes with Instapage, Leadpages, and HubSpot's landing page tools, and differentiates itself primarily through its emphasis on conversion-rate optimization and AI-assisted traffic routing rather than being purely a page-design tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Unbounce
paragraphs: Unbounce is a no-code landing page builder and conversion optimization platform that lets marketers create, publish, and test landing pages, popups, and sticky bars without relying on developers.

It was founded in 2009 in Vancouver, Canada, by Rick Perreault, Carl Schmidt, Oli Gardner, and Jason Murphy, making it one of the earliest dedicated landing page platforms in the marketing technology space.

In 2021, private equity firm Thoma Bravo acquired a majority stake in Unbounce, and the company has continued to operate and expand its product line under that ownership, including leadership changes such as CEO Felicia Bochicchio in 2023.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core builder uses a drag-and-drop editor for landing pages, popups, and sticky bars, with a library of conversion-focused templates spanning industries and campaign types.

Unbounce's built-in A/B testing lets marketers run multiple page variants and measure conversion performance, while its Smart Traffic feature uses AI to automatically send visitors to the variant most likely to convert them based on attributes like device, location, and traffic source.

The platform also supports dynamic text replacement for PPC campaigns, integrations with major marketing and CRM tools, and advanced targeting options including location-based and cookie-based targeting on higher tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Unbounce is priced across three main tiers: Build at 99 dollars per month for unlimited landing pages, popups, and sticky bars with up to 20,000 monthly visitors; Experiment at 149 dollars per month adding unlimited A/B testing; and Optimize at 249 dollars per month adding the AI-driven Smart Traffic feature and advanced targeting.
Annual billing lowers the effective monthly rate on each tier, and all plans include a 14-day free trial before requiring payment.

features.description: Location-based and cookie-based targeting available on higher-tier plans.

pros: AI-driven Smart Traffic can lift conversion rates without manual test analysis

Long track record as one of the original dedicated landing page platforms
Strong template library covering many campaign types and industries
Built-in A/B testing removes the need for a separate testing tool
cons: Pricing is on the higher end compared to some newer landing page competitors
Visitor caps on each tier mean costs rise as traffic grows
Smart Traffic and advanced targeting are locked to the top-tier Optimize plan
Learning curve for advanced customization beyond templates
No permanently free plan; only a 14-day trial

faqs.question: What is Unbounce used for?
faqs.answer: Unbounce is used to build, test, and publish landing pages, popups, and sticky bars for marketing campaigns without coding.

faqs.question: How much does Unbounce cost?
faqs.answer: Unbounce starts at 99 dollars per month for the Build plan, with Experiment at 149 dollars per month and Optimize at 249 dollars per month.

faqs.question: Who founded Unbounce?
faqs.answer: Unbounce was founded in 2009 in Vancouver, Canada, by Rick Perreault, Carl Schmidt, Oli Gardner, and Jason Murphy.

faqs.answer: Smart Traffic is an AI feature that automatically routes visitors to the landing page variant statistically most likely to convert them.

faqs.answer: Yes, Unbounce offers a 14-day free trial on all its pricing plans.

faqs.question: Who owns Unbounce?
faqs.answer: Private equity firm Thoma Bravo acquired a majority stake in Unbounce in 2021.

faqs.answer: Yes, A/B testing is built into the Experiment and Optimize plans, allowing marketers to test multiple page variants.

faqs.question: What are the main Unbounce alternatives?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Instapage, Leadpages, and landing page tools built into platforms like HubSpot.
